description: Deletes a target application pod in a Kubernetes namespace to simulate an unexpected pod failure. This fault introduces a pod-level disruption into the simulation environment, causing the target pod to be terminated and forcing Kubernetes self-healing mechanisms (ReplicaSet reconciliation) to restore the application to a healthy state.

expected_impact.expected_recovery_mechanism: ReplicaSet controller recreates the deleted pod automatically

symptoms: Target pod enters Terminating state and is removed from the cluster
Application endpoint returns HTTP 5xx errors or connection refused during pod downtime
Kubernetes events show pod deletion and ReplicaSet scaling activity
Dependent services may report upstream connection failures or timeouts
fault_description_goal_remediation.goal: Introduce a pod-level disruption into the simulation environment by forcefully deleting the target application pod in the specified namespace, causing service unavailability and triggering Kubernetes self-healing mechanisms (ReplicaSet reconciliation) to restore the pod and resume normal operations.
fault_description_goal_remediation.remediation: Verify that the Kubernetes ReplicaSet controller has automatically recreated the deleted pod. Confirm the new pod reaches Running and Ready status. If the pod fails to restart (e.g., CrashLoopBackOff), investigate container logs and resource constraints. Escalate if self-healing does not restore the application within the expected recovery window.

ideal_course_of_action.detail: Identify that a pod in the target namespace has been terminated unexpectedly by monitoring Kubernetes events, pod status changes, or alerting signals.

ideal_course_of_action.action: Identify the affected pod and namespace
ideal_course_of_action.detail: Determine the exact pod name, namespace, and the owning workload (Deployment/ReplicaSet) that was affected.
Inspect the current pod status to see if it is in Terminating, Pending, or CrashLoopBackOff state. Review Kubernetes events for the namespace to understand the deletion cause.
Confirm that the ReplicaSet or Deployment controller has detected the missing replica and initiated creation of a replacement pod.
Wait for the replacement pod to reach Running and Ready status. Check that all containers within the pod pass readiness and liveness probes.
Verify that the application endpoint is responding correctly and that the Kubernetes Service has registered the new pod's IP in its endpoints.
Summarize the incident: pod was deleted (simulated failure), Kubernetes self-healing restored the pod, service is back to normal. Note time-to-detect and time-to-recover.

ideal_tool_usage_trajectory.purpose: List all pods in the target namespace to identify missing or restarting pods
Get detailed pod information including events, conditions, and container statuses
Review recent Kubernetes events to identify pod deletion and recreation events
Verify that the ReplicaSet has the desired replica count and is reconciling
Check the Deployment status to confirm desired vs available replicas
Inspect logs of the newly created pod to confirm it started successfully without errors
Verify that the Kubernetes Service endpoints include the new pod's IP address
